planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Taiwan toad lily spreads more assertively by stolons than hairy toad lily (T. hirta) and can form substantial colonies in favorable conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade, spacing 18–24 inches apart; Taiwan toad lily is the most commonly grown toad lily in American gardens and is distinguished from the similar hairy toad lily (T. hirta) by its terminal flower clusters (borne at the branch tips, not in the leaf axils), its upright rather than strictly arching stems, and its slightly wider hardiness range extending to zone 9. Tricyrtis formosana produces white to pale lilac flowers heavily spotted with purple and with a yellow-centered throat from August through October, on 18–30 inch stems that may branch near the top; the blooms genuinely resemble miniature orchids and appear when virtually no other shade perennials are in flower. It spreads more freely than T. hirta by underground stolons, gradually forming substantial colonies; divide in early spring every few years to manage spread and rejuvenate clumps. Excellent paired with hostas, Japanese forest grass, and late-season ferns in the shade border.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full shade; macrantha toad lily differs from most toad lilies in bearing nodding, tubular bell-shaped yellow flowers rather than the flat-open, starry flowers typical of the genus — it is an elegant collector's plant best suited to sheltered, humus-rich woodland gardens.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full shade, spacing 18–24 inches apart; macrantha toad lily is a Japanese woodland species that stands apart from most other toad lilies by producing nodding, tubular, bell-shaped flowers rather than the open, starry, orchid-like blooms of the more familiar T. hirta and T. formosana. The pendant, 1-inch yellow flowers are spotted with reddish-brown inside and hang from arching stems in late summer and fall, giving the plant the look of an elegant woodland bell. Tricyrtis macrantha requires consistently moist, humus-rich soil in deep or partial shade and performs best in sheltered, cool woodland gardens; it is less tolerant of drought and heat than the common toad lily and is considered an advanced collector's plant rather than a garden workhorse. Protect from slugs, which can damage the foliage, and provide a light mulch to retain moisture and protect the crowns in colder zones.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Canton fairy bell closely resembles Solomon's seal in form and cultural requirements and makes an elegant, ornamental shade perennial with attractive flowers in spring and colorful berries in fall.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade, spacing 18–24 inches apart; Canton fairy bell is a Chinese ornamental perennial producing elegant, arching stems 2–4 feet tall with lance-shaped, strongly parallel-veined leaves and clusters of 1–3 small, pendant, cream-white bell-shaped flowers at the stem tips and leaf axils in mid-spring. The flowers give way to attractive, round, red to dark-red berries in fall that are ornamentally valuable and provide wildlife food. Disporum cantoniense spreads slowly by rhizomes to form refined colonies and makes an excellent companion to hostas, ferns, and astilbes in the shaded border; in form and habit it closely resembles Solomon's seal (Polygonatum) and can be used in similar situations. Several ornamental cultivars exist, including dark-foliaged forms; all require consistently moist, humus-rich soil and dislike drought.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Korean fairy bell is a refined, smaller-growing shade perennial best suited to cool-climate gardens where it is an outstanding foliage and flower plant for the woodland garden.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade, spacing 12–18 inches apart; Korean fairy bell is a refined, compact shade perennial from Korea and northeast China, producing slender, branching stems 12–18 inches tall with oval, bright-green leaves and solitary, pendant, yellow-green bell-shaped flowers at the stem tips in mid-spring. The flowers give way to single, round, ornamental orange berries in fall that are an attractive fruiting accent in the woodland garden. Disporum uniflorum is an elegant, slow-growing plant that thrives in cool, moist, humus-rich conditions and is best suited to gardens in the northern half of its hardiness range where summer temperatures remain moderate; it is not heat-tolerant and performs poorly in hot, dry summers. Combine with other small-scale shade plants such as wild ginger, bunchberry, and native ferns for a refined woodland effect.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained woodland soil in partial to full shade; perfoliate bellwort is distinguished from the similar large-flowered bellwort (U. grandiflora) by its smaller, paler yellow flowers and by the granular-papillose (roughened) texture on the inner surface of its petals.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained woodland soil in partial to full shade, setting rhizomes about 2 inches deep; perfoliate bellwort is a native spring wildflower of eastern North American deciduous forests that closely resembles the large-flowered bellwort (U. grandiflora) but is distinguished by its smaller, paler, lemon-yellow flowers and by a distinctive granular-roughened texture on the inner surface of its petals visible on close examination. Like U. grandiflora, the stem of U. perfoliata appears to pass directly through the leaves (perfoliate), giving both species their characteristic appearance; however, perfoliate bellwort tends to be somewhat less vigorous and slightly more shade tolerant than its larger relative. It goes dormant by midsummer and should be planted with late-emerging companions such as hostas or ferns to fill the gap. Spreads slowly by rhizomes to form loose colonies and is an excellent naturalizer in shaded native plant gardens from New England to Florida.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring or fall in moist to average, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; sessile bellwort is the most shade-tolerant and cold-hardy of the bellworts and is distinguished from U. perfoliata and U. grandiflora by its sessile (not perfoliate) leaves — the stem does NOT pass through the leaves.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring or fall in moist to average, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; sessile bellwort is the smallest and most cold-hardy of the eastern North American bellworts, reaching only 6–12 inches tall and bearing narrow, pale cream-yellow, pendant flowers on slender stems in April and May. Critically, Uvularia sessilifolia is distinguished from the perfoliate bellworts (U. grandiflora and U. perfoliata) by its sessile leaves — the stem clasps but does NOT pierce through the leaf blade, so the leaves appear to wrap around the stem at the base rather than to be skewered by it. It is more tolerant of dry shade and deeper shade than its relatives and is the best bellwort for difficult, low-light situations; it spreads slowly by rhizomes to form loose, refined colonies. Also known as wild oats for the grass-like appearance of its narrow leaves and pendant seed capsules; goes dormant by midsummer like all bellworts.
